verses.text: The Genealogy of Jesus Christ, son of David, son of Abraham.
Abraham fathered Isaac, Isaac fathered Jacob, Jacob fathered Judah and his brothers.
Judah fathered Perez and Zerah by Tamar, Perez fathered Hezron, Hezron fathered Ram.
Ram fathered Amminadab, Amminadab fathered Nahshon, Nahshon fathered Salmon.
Salmon fathered Boaz by Rahab, Boaz fathered Obed by Ruth, Obed fathered Jesse.
Jesse fathered David the king. David fathered Solomon by the wife of Uriah.
Solomon fathered Rehoboam, Rehoboam fathered Abijah, Abijah fathered Asa.
Asa fathered Jehoshaphat, Jehoshaphat fathered Joram, Joram fathered Uzziah.
Uzziah fathered Jotham, Jotham fathered Ahaz, Ahaz fathered Hezekiah.
Hezekiah fathered Manasseh, Manasseh fathered Ammon, Ammon fathered Josiah.
Josiah fathered Jeconiah and his brothers during the Babylonian exile.
After the Babylonian exile, Jeconiah fathered Shealtiel, Shealtiel fathered Zerubbabel.
Zerubbabel fathered Abiud, Abiud fathered Eliakim, Eliakim fathered Azor.
Azor fathered Zadok, Zadok fathered Achim, Achim fathered Eliud.
Eliud fathered Eleazar, Eleazar fathered Matthan, Matthan fathered Jacob.
Jacob fathered Joseph, the husband of Mary, of whom Jesus was born, who is called Christ.
Thus from Abraham to David there were fourteen generations; from David to the Babylonian exile, fourteen generations; and from the Babylonian exile to Christ, fourteen generations.
This is how the birth of Jesus Christ came about: His mother Mary, legally engaged to Joseph, was found to have a child in her womb from the Holy Spirit before they joined together.
Now Joseph her husband, being a righteous man and not wanting to disgrace her publicly, decided to send her away secretly.
As he pondered these things, an angel of the Lord appeared in a dream, saying, “Joseph, son of David, do not fear to take Mary as your wife, for the child conceived in her is from the Holy Spirit.”
She will bear a son, and you will call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins.
All this happened to fulfill what the Lord had spoken through the prophet, saying,
“Behold, the virgin in her womb will conceive and bear a son and they will call his name Immanuel”, which means ‘With us is God’.
When Joseph awoke from sleep, he did as the angel of the Lord commanded him and took Mary as his wife.
But he did not touch her until she gave birth to a son, and he called his name Jesus.

footnotes.content: Matthew arranges the genealogy in three groups of fourteen generations (Abraham–David; David–Babylonian exile; exile–Christ) for mnemonic and symbolic reasons. The number 14 corresponds to the numeric value of *David* in Hebrew דוד = 4+6+4. He intentionally omits several kings (e.g., Ahaziah, Joash, Amaziah from v. 8) to shape the structure, emphasizing Jesus as the promised son of David and signaling divine design in Israel’s history (cf. 2 Chronicles 22–25). This stylized list follows ancient Jewish literary practices for theological storytelling.

The phrase ‘from the Holy Spirit’ translates the Greek ek pneumatos hagiou, which emphasizes origin rather than association. It points to the conception as truly originating in the Spirit's initiating action—not merely attributed to the Spirit (“of the Holy Spirit”) or performed as an external act (“by the Holy Spirit”), but proceeding directly from the divine source.

The term ‘disgrace’ reflects the severe social and moral consequences in first-century Jewish culture, where public shame was devastating. The Greek deigmatisai implies exposing her publicly, while ‘send her away quietly’ translates apolusai (to release or dismiss). Together, they reveal Joseph’s dilemma and his desire to protect Mary’s dignity with quiet compassion.

The omission of ‘firstborn’ aligns with the earliest and most reliable manuscripts, avoiding potential ambiguity about Mary’s marital status after Jesus’ birth. While later versions include it, this may imply additional children—a theological concern for traditions emphasizing Mary’s perpetual virginity. However, the word ‘until’ is retained, reflecting the Greek heōs hou, which denotes a state up to a specific point without implying change afterward. The phrase ‘did not touch her’ replaces the traditional rendering ‘did not know her’ to avoid ambiguity about sexual union, preserving clarity and poetic tone while adhering to biblical idiom. The Greek egnō refers to intimate knowledge in this context.
